dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 4 for a Sick Person call at [CITY] Address Redacted.
Hi, I'm John, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I've been having pain under my left breast and feeling dizzy.
Ma'am, can you tell me your name and where we are right now?
Jane ... we're in [STATE].
Do you know what day it is?
Saturday, I think. I'm not sure.
Your airway is clear and unobstructed.
You are using your accessory muscles a bit more than usual, but there are no signs of stridor or wheezing.
Pulse is 120, fast and normal in quality.
You mentioned feeling dizzy-have you noticed any fatigue along with that?
The dizziness and pain here started today, and the lump under my left breast has been growing for three days.
What medical conditions have you been diagnosed with in the past?
I have type one diabetes, a history of blood clots in my veins, bipolar disorder, and major depression.
When did you take your last dose of your diabetes medications?
Lantus at 30 at night, Humalog with meals, and my metformin too. No changes in dosing.
When did the pain first become noticeable?
It started three days ago, and it's gotten worse since then.
Does the pain seem to get worse when you move or do anything in particular?
Yes, particularly when I move around or shift positions.
What kind of pain are you experiencing?
It's sharp and throbbing, like I've been hit.
Does the pain radiate or go anywhere else, like your arms or back?
No, the pain is localized under my left breast.
On a scale where 1 is mild and 10 is the worst you've ever felt, how severe is your pain?
I'd say it's about a 7 right now.
Your EKG shows a heart rhythm of sinus tachycardia.
Your pulse is 120 beats per minute.
Your respiratory rate is 26 breaths per minute.
Your blood pressure is 80 over 46 mmHg.
Blood glucose is 534 mg/dL, which is very high.
Your oxygen saturation is 95% on room air.
No chest deformities, and no trouble with breathing.
You seem alert and oriented to person, place, time, and event.
You have a low-grade fever-100.2°F measured at the tympanic area.
Based on vitals and the blood glucose, we're following protocol 3-7 for hyperglycemia.
We'd like to get you stabilized first. Where would you prefer to go?
Please take me to [COMPANY_NAME].
We're now going to start an IV line in your arm and give some fluids-okay?
The IV was inserted successfully in your left anterior cephalic vein.
We are giving 800 mL of normal saline intravenously to help with fluid loss from hyperglycemia.
We've started 4 Lead EKG monitoring for continuous rhythm surveillance.
The EKG is still showing sinus tachycardia after initial interventions.
Your pulse has decreased to 109 BPM, and your BP is now 85 over 43 mmHg.
Your SpO2 is up to 100%, but the blood glucose is still very high and unmeasurable.
We completed the 12 Lead ECG for a more detailed look at your rhythm.
IV fluids continue and we're monitoring your BP closely.
Your respiratory rate is now 28, and BP has improved slightly to 95 over 53 mmHg.
SpO2 has dropped to 87 but has since improved to 94.
The EKG is still showing sinus tachycardia with no other changes detected.
Your pulse is now at 97 BPM and respiratory rate has increased to 33 breaths per minute.
Your pulse is 95 BPM and your BP is 89 over 55 mmHg.
You seem more stable now, Jane. Let's move you carefully to the ambulance.
Thanks, I feel a little stronger now. That helped me out.
We'll get you to [COMPANY_NAME] in ten minutes. I'll monitor you while we're moving.
Let me check in-how is the pain now on a scale of 1 to 10?
I would say it's down to a 5 now. Still there, but less bad.
Have you noticed any changes in your symptoms or any new issues while we've been with you?
No, nothing new. Just the same discomfort from the lump and my diabetes.
No one to notify, as per your request, and no allergies to medications to report.
You're already in the ambulance-so we'll be en route shortly.
Our diagnosis is hyperglycemia secondary to type one diabetes. We followed protocol 3-7 during this call.
Patient is alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation.
Vital signs and IV fluids remained stable during transport. Patient requested to be taken to [COMPANY_NAME].
Securely strapped your safety onto the stretcher. All good to go.
Checking skin for any rashes, bruising, or signs of dehydration-everything looks normal.
Looking at your pupils, they're equal and reactive. No neck tenderness or swelling noted.
No drainage or pain in your ears, nasal areas, or throat-nothing abnormal there.
Pressing gently-no tenderness or rigidity in your abdomen here.
You mentioned increased urination and thirst. Have you had any problem urinating today?
No, I just can't seem to stay hydrated. It's been unusual for me.
Rechecking BP and heart rate-95/53 and 95 respectively. Still no change in heart rhythm.
EKG remains stable. Checking IV site-no swelling, redness, or bleeding noted.
Your IV fluids are running smoothly. I'll check the other 12 Lead EKG again soon.
Do you feel any numbness, tingling, or weakness in your arms or legs?
No, everything seems to be moving and feeling fine for now.
Any pain or tenderness in your back or sides?
No, nothing in the back or flank. The pain is focused on the left breast and I can feel the lump when I press on it.
Blood pressure stable with slight fluctuation. We'll keep monitoring this closely en route.
I'll repeat the EKG and recheck the IV every 15 minutes as protocol.
Things look consistent, Jane. We'll keep everything running smoothly until we arrive at the hospital.
Jane, I can see the lights on [COMPANY_NAME] getting brighter. We'll be there in a few more minutes.
Pain on the same spot today? And still localized?
Still there, but I've been feeling better since the fluids started. Maybe an easier 5 right now.
Your symptoms haven't changed. Blood sugar remains high, and you haven't had access to a glucometer recently?
That's correct. I tried once before this and it didn't read well. I haven't checked since.
Fire truck ahead-we'll be rolling in. I'll move the stretcher slowly to continue care safely.
Ambulance just arrived at destination. Ready to transfer care to receiving facility staff.
You're secured and ready for handoff. Medics, we're handing off a 39-year-old female alert and oriented to all four aspects.
Final check: BP 89/55, pulse 97, Resp 33, SpO2 94.
IV continues and EKG rhythm has not changed.
Making sure IV is flushed and competent.
Meds: Lantus, Humalog, and metformin-no documented changes reported.
We administered 800cc of fluids en route and monitored with continuous EKG as per protocol.
Intravenous fluids are complete-transfusion bag is empty now with IV site functioning well.
Secure and ready for transfer, Jane. I'll signal that we're done here.
No interventions have needed to be halted or restarted at this time.
No further medications needed until receiving facility takes over care at this time.
Reiterating patient is on fluids and EKG monitoring, alert and oriented, and on her way to [COMPANY_NAME].
Patient ready to be transferred-receive care from facilities. Thank you, and enjoy recovery.
